did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically MobileNetV2, MobileNetV3, and VGG19, to classify skin cancer images into Benign, Carcinoma, and Melanoma classes. The study employed transfer learning and evaluated the impact of hair removal preprocessing (Black Top Hat Transform and Wavelet Transform) on the classification accuracy of these AI models using the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the application of Black Top Hat Transform and Wavelet Transform for hair removal in skin cancer images, followed by classification using CNN models (MobileNetV2, MobileNetV3, VGG19) on the ISIC dataset. It reports new empirical results, including specific accuracy metrics (e.g., 92.46% training accuracy for MobileNetV3) and confusion matrices, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Hair Removal Methods in Skin Cancer Images Using Black Top Hat Transform and Wavelet Transform
journal: 2024 Ninth International Conference on Informatics and Computing (ICIC)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of hair interference in skin cancer image classification by proposing a preprocessing method combining Black Top Hat Transform and Wavelet Transform. Using the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset, the researchers evaluated the impact of this hair removal technique on the classification accuracy of three Convolutional Neural Network models: MobileNetV2, MobileNetV3, and VGG19. The evaluation involved comparing model performance on images with and without the proposed preprocessing methods across Benign, Carcinoma, and Melanoma classes. Results indicated that the proposed method improved classification accuracy by 6-20% across all architectures, with MobileNetV3 achieving the highest performance. Specifically, MobileNetV3 attained a training accuracy of 92.46% and a validation accuracy of 90.33% after preprocessing. The study concludes that integrating Black Top Hat Transform and Wavelet Transform significantly enhances the reliability of AI-driven skin cancer detection systems.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three classification categories used in the ISIC dataset subset. Images labeled as benign were used for training, validation, and testing. The study notes that benign tumors are a common type of skin cancer (in the broad sense of skin lesions/tumors) and that benign lesions are non-cancerous.

One of the three classification categories used in the ISIC dataset subset. Described as a 'more aggressive form of skin cancer compared to benign lesions.' Included in the confusion matrix results for all three CNN models.

One of the three classification categories used in the ISIC dataset subset. Described as 'the most hazardous type of skin cancer due to its significant potential for metastasis.' Included in the confusion matrix results for all three CNN models.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories: Benign, Carcinoma, and Melanoma
all_task_types.details: The paper uses Convolutional Neural Networks (MobileNetV2, MobileNetV3, VGG19) to classify skin cancer images into three discrete classes: Benign, Carcinoma, and Melanoma. The study evaluates classification accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score for these categories using the ISIC dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collaboration)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset providing high-quality skin cancer images annotated by dermatologists. The study used a subset of this dataset, featuring images uniformly sized at 224x224 pixels, selected to ensure a balanced representation of various skin cancer categories.

use.training: Used for training the CNN models (MobileNetV2, MobileNetV3, VGG19) with 2400 samples (80% of total).
use.validation: Used for validation with 300 samples (10% of total) to monitor model performance and prevent overfitting.
use.testing: Used for testing with 300 samples (10% of total) to evaluate the final classification accuracy.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance improvements were described using descriptive statistics (accuracy percentages) and comparative analysis of confusion matrices.

ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collabo.notes: The proposed method (Black Top Hat Transform and Wavelet Transform) improved classification accuracy by 6-20% across all architectures. MobileNetV3 achieved the highest performance. VGG19 showed more stable results across both conditions but was less improved than MobileNetV3. MobileNetV2 showed significant improvement but had weaker performance for Melanoma class with proposed methods.

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning from pre-trained models (ImageNet implied for MobileNetV3/VGG19 context, though not explicitly stated as 'ImageNet' in the specific architecture description chunks, standard for these models; MobileNetV3 uses Network Search for architectural design automation)

MobileNetV3.description: Extension of previous version, introduced Network Search for architectural design automation, used hard-swish blocks to replace ReLU activation, integrated SE (Squeeze-and-Excitation) block

MobileNetV2.description: Introduces inverted residual blocks and linear bottlenecks to reduce parameters and computations

VGG19.description: 19 weight layers comprising 16 convolutional layers and 3 fully connected layers. Uses small 3x3 convolutional filters and max pooling with size 2x2. First two fully connected layers contain 4096 units, final layer has 1000 units with softmax

classification_or_output_head.details: VGG19 uses 3 fully connected layers (4096, 4096, 1000 units) with softmax. MobileNetV3 and MobileNetV2 use standard classification heads for 3 classes (Benign, Carcinoma, Melanoma)

architecture_used.approximate_parameters: Thousands to millions (general description for CNNs in paper)

preprocessing_techniques: Black Top Hat Transform: Used to remove hair noise and enhance contrast between darker skin lesions and lighter backgrounds by calculating the difference between the original image and its morphological closing (dilation followed by erosion).

Wavelet Transform (specifically Discrete Wavelet Transform - DWT): Applied for segmentation and noise reduction by decomposing the image into frequency components (LL, LH, HL, HH coefficients) to isolate details and improve image quality.
Image Resizing: Images from the ISIC dataset were uniformly sized to 224x224 pixels.
Data Splitting: The dataset was split into training (80%), validation (10%), and testing (10%) sets.
conclusions: The integration of Black Top Hat Transform and Wavelet Transform significantly enhances the quality of skin lesion images for segmentation and classification by effectively mitigating hair noise, improving contrast, and capturing intricate details across multiple scales.
The proposed hair removal method improves the classification accuracy of Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models by 6-20% across all tested architectures (MobileNetV2, MobileNetV3, and VGG19).
MobileNetV3 achieved the highest performance among the tested models, with a training accuracy of 92.46% and a validation accuracy of 90.33% after applying the proposed preprocessing methods.

MobileNetV3 demonstrated superior accuracy and generalization capabilities compared to MobileNetV2 and VGG19, while VGG19 was found to be more prone to overfitting due to its architectural complexity.
The presence of hair in skin cancer images presents significant challenges for effective learning in CNN models, as evidenced by lower accuracy scores when hair removal techniques were not applied.
The implementation of suitable image processing techniques combined with efficient classification models markedly improves the accuracy and reliability of AI-driven skin cancer detection systems.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, custom subset of the ISIC dataset (3,000 images total: 2,400 training, 300 validation, 300 testing) rather than the full standard ISIC splits, which limits the generalizability of the results to broader dermatological populations.

The dataset was selected to ensure a 'balanced representation' of classes, which may not reflect the real-world prevalence of skin cancer types (e.g., melanoma is typically rarer than benign lesions in general populations), potentially inflating performance metrics for minority classes.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance improvements (6-20%) are reported using descriptive statistics only, making it impossible to determine if the gains are statistically significant.

The paper does not report external validation on independent datasets or cross-validation strategies beyond the single train/val/test split, raising concerns about overfitting to the specific subset and lack of robustness assessment.

MobileNetV2 showed significant performance disparity between classes with the proposed method (e.g., Melanoma F1-score of 66.09% vs. Carcinoma 91.43%), indicating potential bias or instability in detecting specific lesion types despite overall accuracy gains.

VGG19 was noted to be 'more prone to overfitting due to its architectural complexity,' yet it was included in the final evaluation without explicit regularization techniques (beyond the proposed preprocessing) to mitigate this known risk in deep networks.

The preprocessing pipeline (Black Top Hat Transform and Wavelet Transform) was applied uniformly; the paper does not discuss the impact of varying hair density or lesion sizes on the efficacy of the hair removal, which could introduce bias in clinical deployment.
The study is a conference paper (ICIC 2024) with limited scope, lacking detailed hyperparameter tuning reports, loss function specifications, or optimizer details, which hinders reproducibility.
